title: Relightable Gaussian Splatting for Indoor Scenes from Casual Monocular Captures
teaser: Recent relightable 3D Gaussian methods rely on controlled light-stage data or specialize to human heads, leaving room-scale scenes from casual handheld video largely unsolved. This project replaces light-stage supervision with pretrained 2D priors (monocular normals, intrinsic-image diffusion, open-vocabulary segmentation) and treats the choice of per-Gaussian material model as an open question — comparing analytical microfacet BRDFs, neural shaders, hybrid neural-analytical formulations, and radiance-transfer parameterizations on full indoor scenes to identify the best trade-off between relighting fidelity, editability, and trainability under prior-guided supervision.
abstract: Reconstructing relightable 3D scenes from casual captures is a central open problem in inverse rendering. Relightable 3D Gaussian Splatting has shown impressive results on head avatars and object-centric benchmarks, but scaling to general indoor scenes is hindered by the absence of controlled illumination, the variety of materials at scene scale, and strong cast shadows that a per-Gaussian appearance model cannot easily absorb. This project investigates whether pretrained 2D priors — monocular geometry, intrinsic-image decomposition, and semantic segmentation — can substitute for light-stage supervision when decomposing a handheld phone video into a relightable 3D Gaussian scene. A central question is which per-Gaussian material parameterization is best suited to this prior-guided regime: under a shared pipeline we compare analytical microfacet BRDFs, fully neural shaders, hybrid neural-analytical formulations, and radiance-transfer parameterizations. Quantitative evaluation is performed on synthetic indoor benchmarks with full ground truth (e.g. FIPT, Hypersim, InteriorVerse), with qualitative results on real captures from ScanNet++ and MipNeRF-360.
milestones: Reproduce a relightable 3DGS baseline and set up a quantitative evaluation protocol on a synthetic indoor benchmark with full ground truth.
Integrate monocular geometry and intrinsic-image diffusion priors into the optimization to disentangle albedo, normals, and roughness without light-stage supervision.
Compare per-Gaussian material models — analytical, neural, hybrid, and radiance-transfer — under the prior-guided pipeline.
Extend the best model with importance-sampled specular reflections and a learned visibility term, and benchmark against single-illumination 3DGS baselines.

prerequisites.label: Advanced Deep Learning for Computer Vision: Visual Computing (ADL4CV)
